title: Unpacking the Packaged: Ultra-Processed Foods and Your Heart
text:
A very warm welcome to everyone joining
us from different parts of the world. We
are delighted to have you with us for
today's webinar titled unpacking the
packaged ultrarocessed foods and your
heart. So I'll start by briefly setting
the context for today's
discussion. We know that India's
healthcape has been shifting rapidly and
that makes today's topic even more
relevant than ever. While we continue to
address the burden of communicable
diseases, the growing challenge of
non-communicable diseases demands urgent
attention. According to WH 2022
estimates, India accounts for 2/3 of all
NCD related deaths in the Southeast Asia
region. And despite proactive efforts by
the government, shifting dietary
patterns and rising consumption of
ultrarocessed foods continues to worsen
the NCD
burden. While traditionally most dietary
discussions have focused on
malnutrition, their role in driving NCDs
is now widely recognized. In fact, the
recent global burden of disease study
identified poor dietary habits including
high salt, trans fats and processed food
intake as key risk factors for NCD
mortality in India. So to strengthen the
focus on this important issue and drive
strategic action, the Harvard Thchan
School of Public Health India Research
Center is launching a webinar series,
the impact of diets on non-communicable
diseases, evidence and the next steps.
This series will convene national and
global experts to discuss the evidence,
identify gaps and share strategies to
strengthen public health responses in
India. Today's session marks the
inaugural event in this series and we
are truly honored to be joined by an
esteemed panel who will share their
insights on how we can tackle this
epidemic.
My name is Swati Bakshi and I serve as
the deputy director of the Harvard
Thchan School of Public Health India
Research Center. Established in 2015,
our center is the first global center of
the Harvard Shan School working to
advance health in India and globally
through research, training and capacity
building and knowledge translation and
communication. Our center is led by Dr.
K. Vishwanat Lee Kangi Professor of
Health Communication at Harvard Thchan
School of Public
Health. This webinar is co-hosted along
with Anuad Solutions led by Dr. Ana
Aasti. Anubad is an accelerator for
knowledge translation designed to
translate the latest scientific evidence
into actionable policy insights for
advancing India's nutrition agenda. And
now it's my absolute privilege to
introduce our distinguished, highly
respected and accomplished
panelists. Firstly, Dr. Avin Santla. So
he's joining us today in place of Dr.
Swasti Sharon who is unable to attend
due to a lastm minute meeting at the
ministry. So Dr. Slia serves as the
deputy assistant director general at
diretory general of health services
under the ministry of health and family
welfare government of India. He's a
public health professional with more
than 11 years of experience working
across various levels of the health
system in India. He has been involved
with various national health programs
including the tobacco control program,
non-communicable diseases, climate
change and human health, integrated
disease surveillance program, health
care of elderly to name a few. Welcome
sir. It's a pleasure to have you with us
today.
Next we have Dr. Frank Hu. Dr. Hu is the
Frederick Jstster Professor of Nutrition
and Epidemiology and Chair of the
Department of Nutrition at the Harvard
Thchan School of Public Health. He also
serves as professor of medicine at
Harvard Medical School and Grigum and
Women's Hospital.
Its major research interests include
epidemiology and prevention of
cardioabolic diseases through diet and
lifestyle, gene environment interactions
and risk of obesity and type 2 diabetes,
nutritional metabolomics and many more.
Doctor Who serves as director of dietary
biomarker development center and
co-director of obesity epidemiology and
prevention program at Harvard. Welcome
Dr. Who. We are delighted to have you
today. Next, I would like to welcome Dr.
Utaro Satoya. Dr. Satoya is a team lead
for NCDS and co-orbidities at WH India
country office specializing in public
health. He has over 20 years of
experience in health research and health
system
development. Before moving to India, he
served as country license officer at the
WHO Tonga office supporting the
government of Tonga and held roles at WH
division of Pacific Technical Support in
Fiji and the Department of Mental Health
and Substance Abuse at WHO
headquarters. Welcome Dr. Satoya. We are
so glad to have you join us. Last but
certainly not the least, Dr. Minakshi
Sharma. Dr. Sharma is scientist G at the
Indian Council of Medical Research
leading work on cardiovascular diseases
and
neurosciences. Her research focuses on
hypertension, ST elevated myioardial
inffection called STEMI and stroke.
She spearheaded the India hypertension
control initiative which was a
nationwide primary healthcare
intervention and has now been integrated
into India's national program for
NCDS. She has also played a key role in
developing STEMI and stroke care models
for rural and remote settings with a
STEMI model now incorporated into
national program guidelines and adopted
by several states. Welcome Dr. Sharma.
It's a pleasure to have you with us.
Well, this was only a brief glimpse into
the remarkable work and achievements of
our esteemed panelists. Each of them
brings a distinguished record and wealth
of experience and I could not possibly
do full justice to their contribution in
such a short introduction. I'm sure they
will share more about their work during
this
discussion. Today's session will be
moderated by Dr. Cindy Leang. She's an
assistant professor of public health
nutrition at the Harvard Thchan School
of Public
Health. Trained as a nutrition
epidemiologist, her research broadly
focuses on diet related health
disparities in structurally marginalized
populations. Thank you Dr. Leang for
joining us today. So the flow is like
this. The panel discussion will run for
about 40 minutes followed by 10 minutes
for audience questions and then closing
remarks by Dr. Ana Aasti. I encourage
all part all attendees to please post
their questions in the Q&A box. Our team
at the back end will collect these
questions and they will assist Dr. Leang
in the discussion. With that, I'm
pleased to hand it over to Dr. Leang to
lead today's discussion. Over to you,
Dr. Leang.
Thank you, Swati. And hello everyone.
I'm pleased to be the moderator for
today's webinar. Um, in in the time that
we find ourselves in right now as a
Harvard community, I think this webinar
really highlights the power of our
global community, particularly as we
find our uh common passions in promoting
healthy food systems for all. So today's
webinar is called unpacking the packaged
ultrarocessed foods in your heart. Now
given the ubiquity of ultrarocessed
foods, I think that we have all seen,
purchased, and probably consumed
ultrarocessed foods at some time in our
life. So this is really an issue that
affects all of us and our families and
our communities. In the past 10 to 15
years, there's been an avalanche of
research really demonstrating the
potential health effects of consuming
too much ultrarocessed foods. So, I'm
really excited to start the conversation
with our esteemed panelists today. We're
going to be ambitious and cover a lot of
important topics including the
prevalence and scale of non-communicable
diseases, the cardiovascular effects of
ultrarocessed foods, enhancing public
health awareness of these products, um,
and also best practices and
evidence-based strategies for promoting
healthy diets and cardiovascular disease
outcomes in India.
So, as Swati said, if you have questions
that come up in the next 40 minutes or
so, please add them to the Q&A button at
the bottom of your Zoom box. We will be
compiling these questions and then um
we'll be able to ask them during the Q&A
session in the last 10
minutes. So, with that, I will um start
the questions with you Dr. Slia. Um,
India has a rising burden of
cardiovascular disease particularly in
the younger
population. What policy measures and
regulatory strategies are currently in
place to address these dietary risk
factors?
Yeah, greetings from uh
uh greetings to the esteemed panelists
and the audience who have joined in for
this uh particular webinar. And I shall
like to start from the 19th April liver
day theme wherein we had this
uh food is medicine as the I would say
as a slogan or the quote which was
popularized across and uh of course uh
our webinar also when we say unpacking
the package and we talk about the
harmful effects of processed food
ultrarocessed food of course and we also
So consider them as sin goods along with
salt and sugar. So of course it is it is
I would say the need of the
R
to raise awareness among the younger
population population regarding the
harmful effects which the ultrarocessed
foods have in the world wherein there is
lot of advertising also and social media
and of course by means of
uh movies or TVs or whatever television
they are trying to popularize that.
whole lot of measures are necessary and
I think basically your question
primarily focuses on the policy measures
and regulatory strategies. Of course the
policy measures and regulatory
strategies they are both our regulatory
strategies are both in terms of uh
strengthening the monitoring and
evaluation component and at the same
time I would use the word supportive
supervision wherein we find out the
faults wherein in the system and then we
try to rectify them and of course our
policy basically it focuses on
the raising awareness raising awareness
on the harmful effects of ultra
processed food. first. Secondly,
discouraging
it targeting the younger
population
to adopt healthy lifestyles and how they
can how they can through by means of
behavior change they can adopt it as a
part of their lifestyle so that they
don't end up with cardiovascular
diseases or I would use the word NCDS
for that matter because apart from
tobacco and alcohol
Unhealthy diet is one of the major risk
factors as whatever research studies
have been conducted here in India. So
unhealthy diet is one of the biggest
risk factors which contributes to NCDS.
So and that could be to the tune of 20
to 25% of the cases are attributed to an
unhealthy diet. So we need to have it.
So the through the whole of society
approach or whole of government approach
uh acro and a robust intersectoral
coordination we have a ecosystem wherein
uh we promote health promote we
encourage health promotion and behavior
change
and discourage the consumption of foods
that are rich in fat, sugar, salt. Of
course uh we
have certain
uh movements or flagship programs such
as eat right India movement is there
wherein we promote just to site an
example that the traditional foods which
used to be there in the households of
course in India is a very primitive
civilization over the years there have
been lot of food habits which have
continued over the years. So
maybe they should stick to that. Uh
then with respect to the behavior change
communication we have the fit India
movement wherein we promote the health
promotion activities marathon or yoga
activities particularly in the younger
population. Our target is that when when
I talk about the of course the uh early
detection and screening component I have
mentioned which we do it at something
known as Aayush manager health and
wellness center. So there are some
centers which are there at the 5,000
population for every 5,000 we have one
wellness center. So the uh community
health officer his main role is to uh
promote and help the population adapt to
health promotion measures and physical
activities and all that. If I have to
specifically mention of of course there
is no need to mention that we have we do
we have a regulatory body of course
every country has it we do also have it
with the name of food facade the name as
FSSAI and uh there is a ban on
industrial
transparenc front front of back labeling
everything is there and school and youth
focused policies are there
and with the setting approach uh we also
try to target the schools, the colleges
and we call it as healthy
campus. The name what we have given is
that healthy campus or setting approach
wherein we
encourage taking healthy food. to eat
right campus that is something uh so all
this is there that is yeah a whole lot
of
thank you thank you for that very
comprehensive response it's uh really
helpful to hear about the whole society
whole population approach but then to to
see all the ways and all the uh age
groups that um are being targeted by
these prevention efforts let me move now
to Dr.
Um, Dr. Who, can you tell us a little
bit more about what ultrarocessed foods
are? What categories are of
ultrarocessed foods are might be more
harmful for human health and how do they
influence metabolic pathways related to
cardiovascular disease?
Hey, uh, thank you Cindy and uh, good
morning, good, good evening everyone. Uh
it's truly a pleasure and a privilege to
participate in this uh panel discussion
about ultrarocessed foods. I think this
is a very timely topic, very important
topic. uh as we all know uh
cardiovascular disease is the leading
cause of death in India and responsible
for nearly onethird of total deaths and
also uh CBD is increasing both urban and
rural areas and heart disease tend to
occur um in younger populations uh in
India compared to western populations
and we also know that
um India is is considered as a capital
of type 2
diabetes. Almost 100 million uh people
in India are living with diabetes and
another major trend is increase in uh
obesity especially abdominal obesity in
both urban and rural areas in India. So
in terms of diet, we know that poor diet
is now a leading cause of deaths uh
globally and surpassing uh tobacco
smoking and um ultrarocessed foods has
uh received tremendous amount of
attention in the past uh 10 to uh 15
years uh because of increasing research
on the harmful effects of uh UPFS. Uh so
I'm going to spend uh a few minutes just
to uh uh present uh our recent uh
studies on ultral processed foods and
the risk of cardiovascular disease. Uh
let me share uh my screen. Uh can you
see it?
Yes, we can see your screen. Okay. Yeah.
So the work I'm going to present it was
conducted by a former student, PhD
student in our department. Kenny Mundaza
uh he was working with me and another
faculty member in our department um Jose
Matai so his entire thesis was uh focus
on uh UPF's um type 2 diabetes and
cardiovascular disease so today I'm
going to present um one of his sister's
papers focus on cardiovascular disease
so this paper was recently published one
of the uh Nancet journals
So this uh study was um conducted in uh
um in our three uh large cohort studies
the cell study uh the cell study 2 and
the health professional follow-up study.
So uh typically um UPFS are classified
based on the uh LOVA uh framework. So
this uh framework has been widely used
to classify food products based on the
extent of processing uh applied to them.
So um according to this framework there
are four categories of foods unprocessed
or minimal processed food uh like apples
uh pears and fruits and veget other
fruits and vegetables uh milk and then
uh the second category is the processed
culinary ingredients like butter uh
vegetable oil uh or or table sugar. And
then the third category is the processed
foods. And the fourth one is
ultrarocessed food or UPFS. So
ultrarocessed foods uh has uh received
most attention because uh uh those are
the foods uh industrially uh uh produced
foods which which are very high in um
unhealthy ingredients such as added
sugar, sodium and unhealthy fats. and
also uh numerous additives and and other
uh ingredients and the consumption of
UPFS has increased uh dramatically
worldwide in the US uh more than uh
twothirds of the calories consumed by
the US population especially by uh US
children come from uh
UPFS and so in this uh study
um Kenny uh specifically analyzed the
associations between UPF consumption and
the risk of CVD including uh coronary
heart disease and stroke in cohort
studies and then he conducted a
systematic review and meta analysis to
update the evidence on the associations
of UPFS with uh those outcomes. So those
are the uh three uh large ongoing
cohorts uh been still being conducted at
Harvard School Public Health and the
Brian Women's Hospital. the nurses
health study uh nurses health study 2
and health professional follow-up study.
So with the three cohorts combined uh we
have almost 200,000 participants who
have been followed for several decades.
Um dietary information has been
collected using validated photo
frequency questions uh every four years.
So the repeated measures of diet has
been very useful in terms of reducing
measurement errors and also um
representing long-term dietary habits.
Uh there are multiple categories of
UPFS. So uh here we have 10 different
categories uh uh highly processed bread,
cereals, um um sauces, spreads, sweet uh
snacks, desserts, several snacks, uh
sugary beverages, um and processed
meats, uh yogurt, um ready to eat uh
meat, uh meals, hard liquor, and
artificially sweetened uh beverages.
So the outcomes uh include um incident
CVD uh during the follow-up um we have
lung fatal MI fd and non fatal and fatal
stroke and total CVD and then the
analysis um was uh conducted using um
Cox regression model adjusted for wide
range of cardiovascular risk factors and
then the meta analysis is met review was
conducted following the prisma
uh
guidelines and um here um the mean
intake of UPFS in our cohort is uh
somewhat lower than in the general
population. So we have about uh 30 to
40% of calories come from UPFS and then
um UPF consumption is associated with
increased energy intake, decreased uh
diet quality and increased uh prevalence
of
obesity and those are the main results
for CBD. So as you can see that in the
uh order analysis of our three cohorts
um higher consumption of uh uh total
UPFS is associated with uh significant
elevated risk of total CVD CHD but not
stroke. So comparing the highest
quintile with the lowest quintile there
is 11% increased CVD risk and the 16%
increased CHD
risk. And then uh when we look at uh
specific categories of UPFS uh the
results are quite hoggenous. So for some
of the UPF categories for example sugary
beverages, processed meats uh there is a
clear uh significant uh positive
association between higher consumption
of those uh foods and the increased CVD
risk. However, for other categories uh
such as uh breakfast cereals, especially
coat cereals and whole wheat bread um
and um and also yogurt uh there is a
moderate uh inverse association between
high consumption of those foods and the
risk of CVD. So clearly um not all UPFs
are created equal and the associations
between the UPFS and the CVD depend on
uh different um
categories. And then uh this meta
analysis included um uh uh 22 uh
perspective cohort studies which include
the three cohort the results from the
three cohorts that I just mentioned. And
as you can see that overall um there is
a significant positive association
between um uh uh UPF consumption and
CVD. So the highest cate comparing the
highest category with the lowest
category there there is a 17% increase
CVD risk. And this association was uh
statistically significant and uh this is
also true for uh CHD about 23% increased
risk for stroke um the association was
uh lower. It's about 9% increased risk.
So uh overall um there is a significant
uh positive association between total
UPF consumption and the risk of
increased risk of CVD. However, uh UPF
subgroups uh uh presented divergent
associations. So clearly the worst type
of um UPFS soft drinks and animal based
uh uh products especially processed
meats. However, some categories of UPFS
such as uh whole wheat bread, cooked
breakfast cereals, yogurt, dairy
dessert, and uh and some um alcohol
beverages actually associated with lower
risk of CVD. So I think uh the take-h
home message is that uh UPF as a overall
category is certainly detrimental for
cardiovascular health but um I think
it's um important to uh keep in mind
that uh the uh UPF categories are very
uh hogenous in terms of uh the different
type of foods and also uh and their uh
potential health effects. So um I think
that's all I want to present and uh let
me pass this to uh Cindy.
Thank you Dr. Who. I think uh that's a
really important message for us to keep
in mind as we're continuing to go
through today's webinar that not all
UPFs are created equal. Some of them are
obviously worse for health as uh Kenny's
data has showed us and some are
actually, you know, not as bad as we
might have initially thought. Um, and it
just also just shows there's so many
different products that go into this
categorization of ultrarocessed foods.
It's not necessarily, you know, the
really brightly packaged foods with the
characters that you see on the
billboards. It really encompasses a lot.
And it's important to be able to tease
out which of these foods are actually
most harmful for cardiovascular
disease. So let me move now to Dr.
Satoya. Um Dr. Satoya, based on global
evidence, what are some effective
population level strategies that have
success successfully reduced

countries? And can you take some of

for India?
Yeah, thank you. Thank you Cindy for the

mentioned I think it's came in a very
good timing because India is facing a

to unhealthy foods urbanization is

and uh as uh Dr. Amina summarized
there's a lot of things already

uh and who has been supporting but yeah
there is a a few things we could think
about
uh in terms of reducing the unhealthy

understand it's India
is is a big country it's like a
continent
uh there's you know so each state is

there's a lot one single intervention

uh in at a small scale so as who we are

that
um these are
uh as a public health specialist as a

but it needs to make sure that these are

So for that who has been uh there's so

say there's too many but if I say one

of NCDS so list uh uh evidence about how

based on the literature and evidence. So
uh in that we have a a number of uh

um recommendation to be you know

this contextualize the cultural

communication
materials behavioral changing

pre-trade was there and um by

uh sorry have diabetes blood pressure

they had
